titleStmt.title: Letter from M C Seton to Robert Chalmers, 5 July 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: M C Seton

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A letter from M C Seton to Robert Chalmers asking for information concerning 'ex-military criminal lunatic James Coghlan'. Sir Robert Chalmers (1858 - 1938) was sent to Ireland as replacement under-secretary for Sir Matthew Nathan, who had resigned along with Augustine Birrell, his chief secretary, following the outbreak of the Easter Rising in 1916.

correspAction.placeName: India Office, Whitehall, London, S.W., London, England

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
INDIA OFFICE,
WHITEHALL,
LONDON, S.W. 5 July 1916. Sir, With reference to Sir Edward O'Farrell's letter No.
20278 dated the 13th December last regarding the discharge
from the Dundrum Criminal Lunatic Asylum of the ex-military
criminal lunatic James Coghlan, I am directed by the
Secretary of State for India in Council to forward for
the information of their Excellencies the Lords Justices
copy of a despatch from the Government of India in which
they ask to be informed, with reference to the provisions
of sub-section 1 of section 10 of the Colonial Prisoners
Removal Act 1884, of the powers possessed by the authorities
in the United Kingdom with regard to the discharge of
European criminals removed from India and of the circumstances
in which these powers can be exercised. Mr.Secretary Chamberlain would be glad to receive in
due course the observations of the Lords Justices on these
points. A similar enquiry is being addressed to the Home Office
and the Scottish Office with regard to the cases of European
criminals removed to England and Wales and to Scotland,
respectively. I have the honour to be, Sir,
Your obedient Servant, M.C. Seton The Under Secretary,
Dublin Castle.
No. 8 OF 1916.
GOVERNMENT OF INDIA.
HOME DEPARTMENT.
MEDICAL.
Jails. To
THE RIGHT HONOURABLE AUSTEN CHAMBERLAIN,
His Majesty's Secretary of State for India.
Simla, the 25th May, 1916. SIR, We have the honour to invite a reference to your despatch no. 20 (Public),
dated the 4th February, 1916, forwarding certain papers regarding the discharge
from the Dundrum Lunatic Asylum of the criminal lunatic James Coghlan,
and in connection therewith to bring the following points to your notice. 2. James Coghlan was convicted of murder and sentenced to death by the
Punjab Chief Court in 1910. The sentence was commuted by order of the local
Government to one of penal servitude for life on the ground that the prisoner
had become insane, and had he remained in a lunatic asylum in India, he
would on becoming sane have been remanded to jail to serve the re-
mainder of his sentence. It is obviously undesirable that European criminals
sent home from India under the provisions of the Colonial Prisoners Removal
Act, 1884, should meet with appreciably more lenient treatment than they
would have received in India in common with other European and Indian
prisoners, and if, as appears to be the case, the authorities in the United
Kingdom have power to order the premature discharge of certain classes of
such prisoners without our knowledge or consent, we apprehend that it may be
necessary for us to exercise considerable caution in availing ourselves of our
powers under the Act. We would ask therefore, that we may be informed,
with reference to the provisions of sub-section 1 of section 10 of the Act, of the
powers possessed by the authorities in the United Kingdom in this respect and
of the circumstances in which these powers can be exercised. On receipt of
this information we will consider our position in the matter further. We have the honour to be,
SIR,
Your most obedient, humble Servants, CHELMSFORD.
BEAUCHAMP DUFF.
R. H. CRADDOCK.
W. S. MEYER.
C. H. A. HILL.
C. SANKARAN NAIR.
G. R. LOWNDES.
G. S. BARNES.
No.
GOVERNMENT OF INDIA.
HOME DEPARTMENT.
MEDICAL.
Jails.
No. 8, DATED SIMLA, THE 26TH MAY, 1916.
(Copy.) LETTER TO HIS MAJESTY'S SECRETARY OF STATE
FOR INDIA SUBJECT:
Discharge from the Dundrum Lunatic Asylum
of the criminal lunatic James Coghlan.
